coredata.dc:title: N-doped carbon quantum dots enhance anaerobic digestion under light condition: The performances and potential mechanisms
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Qiang
Chen, Shuo
Zhang, Haoyu
Feng, Lu
Zhou, Hongjun
Pan, Junting
Li, Yeqing
Xu, Chunming
coredata.dc:description: The treatment of organic waste (OW) by anaerobic digestion (AD) conforms to the concept of sustainable development. But AD is facing the issue of low conversion rate. In this work, the photo-AD system using visible light (LED lamp) as the source was constructed and the performances and mechanism of N-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQD) were explored in the system for the first time. The results showed that 0.5 g/L NCQD promoted a 23.1 % increase in cumulative CH4 yield in the photo-AD system. Microbial analysis results showed that in photo-AD with NCQD, the dominant strain was Methanosarciniales, with an abundance of 69.0 %. Microbial activity and structural integrity tests showed that the microorganisms were not damaged by free radicals. In addition, NCQD increased the redox peak intensity of the CV curve and increased photocurrent intensity of photo-AD. Furthermore, it promoted an increase of 18.2 % (0.26 ± 0.03 μmol/mL) in ATP concentration. The photoelectrochemical analysis and quantitative analysis of functional genes results indicated that NCQD mainly promoted methanogenesis by providing photoelectrons. This promotion mechanism increased the copynumber (61,652.8 g−1) of EchA in photo-AD, rather than Vht and Hdr related to cytochrome. This work provided new strategies for the enhancement of AD and clarified potential mechanisms.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photo-anaerobic digestion
N-doped carbon quantum dots
Microbial community
Methanogenesis
Quantitative PCR

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Green and Sustainable Science and Technology

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Design and operation of experimental setup for photo-AD
Physicochemical characteristics and photochemical properties of NCQD
The mechanism of NCQD on photo-AD promotion

State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil Processing

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22278435), the Strategic Cooperation Technology Projects of CNPC and CUPB (ZLZX2020-04), the Project of the Joint Research Institute of Carbon Neutrality, State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil Processing (CUPB-SH-2-02).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Photo-AD experimental device. (a) Modified BMP system with (b) dark fermentation unit and (c) photo fermentation unit. The schematic diagrams of (d) dark and (e) photo bioreactor.

Characteristics of NCQD. (a) – (b) TEM images of NCQD. (c) The particle size distribution of NCQD. (d) – (e) HRTEM images of NCQD. (f) AFM image of NCQD. High-resolution (g) C 1 s, (h) N 1 s, (i) O 1 s peaks and fitting curves of NCQD.

Visible light responsiveness of NCQD. (a) UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectrum of NCQD. (b) I-t curves of NCQD with a light on/off cycle (60/60 s). (c) Mott-Schottky plot of NCQD. (d) EIS spectra of NCQD.

Methane yield from photo-AD or dark-AD. (a) Cumulative CH4 yield and (b) daily CH4 yield in different bioreactors. (c) Cumulative CH4 yield at the end of the batch BMP experiment. *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001.

The species, proportion, and overall activity of microorganisms. (a) Bacterial community structure at the class level and (b) archaea community structure at the order level. (c) ESR spectra of •OH trapped DMPO with light on 10 min. CLSM images of flocs in (d) dark AD (R) and (e) photo-AD (RL NCQD). Nucleic acids exhibit green fluorescence, after staining with SYTO 9.

The effect of NCQD on methanogenesis in photo-AD system. (a) CV curves and (b) impedances of substrate in different systems. (c) The effect of light on the current in different systems. (d) The concentration of ATP in different systems. (e) DNA concentration and (f) mean copynumber of functional genes in different systems. (g) Schematic diagram of NCQD promoting methanogenesis in photo-AD system.

head.ce:title: N-doped carbon quantum dots enhance anaerobic digestion under light condition: The performances and potential mechanisms

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil Processing
College of Chemical Engineering and Environment

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil Processing, College of Chemical Engineering and Environment, Beijing 102249, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The treatment of organic waste (OW) by anaerobic digestion (AD) conforms to the concept of sustainable development. But AD is facing the issue of low conversion rate. In this work, the photo-AD system using visible light (LED lamp) as the source was constructed and the performances and mechanism of N-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQD) were explored in the system for the first time. The results showed that 0.5 g/L NCQD promoted a 23.1 % increase in cumulative CH4 yield in the photo-AD system. Microbial analysis results showed that in photo-AD with NCQD, the dominant strain was Methanosarciniales, with an abundance of 69.0 %. Microbial activity and structural integrity tests showed that the microorganisms were not damaged by free radicals. In addition, NCQD increased the redox peak intensity of the CV curve and increased photocurrent intensity of photo-AD. Furthermore, it promoted an increase of 18.2 % (0.26 ± 0.03 μmol/mL) in ATP concentration. The photoelectrochemical analysis and quantitative analysis of functional genes results indicated that NCQD mainly promoted methanogenesis by providing photoelectrons. This promotion mechanism increased the copynumber (61,652.8 g−1) of EchA in photo-AD, rather than Vht and Hdr related to cytochrome. This work provided new strategies for the enhancement of AD and clarified potential mechanisms.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: The most significant proportion of solid waste in the world is organic waste (OW), which accounts for about 46 % according to the Energy Information Administration (EIA) [1]. In 2020, there are approximately 235.12 billion tons of municipal solid waste (MSW) and 55.73 billion tons of sewage sludge collected in China, while MSW contains 50 % of food waste as documented in the China Statistical Yearbook 2021 [2]. The huge reserves and increment of OW bring about significant challenges. Therefore, the treatment, disposal or direct utilization of OW has received widespread attention [3].

Anaerobic digestion (AD) is an environmentally friendly technology for the utilization of OW. However, AD has a relatively low conversion rate and long fermentation time, which limits its application. Therefore, conductive materials, including granular activated carbon [4], biochar [5], carbon felt [6] and zero-valent iron (ZVI) [7], etc., were proposed to enhance AD performance, as they could promote direct interspecies electron transfer (DIET). Semiconductors, however, have not been used for AD due to their poor conductivity. In recent years, some studies proposed to use semiconductors to construct biotic-abiotic hybrids [8,9], that converted luminous energy into electrons which were further used by methanogen (Methanosarcina barkeri) to convert CO2 to CH4 [9]. This provided a theoretical basis for utilizing semiconductors to promote AD. In addition, it was reported that light, as an environmental factor, can affect the process of AD [10]. For the AD process, light exposure not only promoted cell aggregation but also the secretion of extracellular polymeric substances (EPS), which were key factors in boosting aggregation and electron transfer [10,11]. Therefore, introducing semiconductors into the AD system with light exposure could potentially promote AD performance and bring synergy impact.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) represent a class of nanocarbon materials exhibiting photo response and many potential applications [12]. It has unique chemical and electronic properties due to the existence of dielectric confinement effect and edge effects [13]. Compared with metal-based materials, CQD has superior biocompatibility and does not have biological toxicity [14,15]. Additionally, the CQD synthesis process is simple and can be obtained from a various carbon resources, such as citric acid [16], glucose, straw, chitosan, and citrus lemon juice [17]. Recent years, CQD have demonstrated promising performance across a large variety of technical practices, e.g., light emission, photo/electro-catalysis, sensing of heavy metal ions, and biological applications of in vivo imaging and antitumor treatment [18–20]. As of now, application of CQD on AD with light exposure (photo-AD) remains unexplored. Insight into the impact and mechanism of CQD addition to photo-AD is the key to promoting its future application.

In this study, we proposed using N-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQD) to enhance photo-AD (LED light) and explored the impact and potential mechanisms. On the basis of biochemical methane potential (BMP) testing system, a photo-AD experimental device was constructed using a LED lamp to provide light for the fermentation unit. The promotion effect of NCQD on photo-AD was investigated through batch AD experiments. The microbial community structure revealed the effects of light and NCQD on the abundance of dominant strain in the system. The detection of free radical and cell integrity investigated the effects of light and NCQD on microorganism activity. Furthermore, ATP content, specific gene, and photoelectrochemical properties of AD substrates were measured, which were used to elucidate the mechanism of NCQD promoting photo-AD. The results of this work provided reference for the use of photoelectron materials to promote AD and reveal the mechanism.

NCQD was prepared using a one-step hydrothermal method [21]. Specifically, sodium citrate (1.0 g) and urea (0.3 g) were dissolved in 50 mL of deionized water, which was transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless-steel reactor and heated at 190 °C for 500 min. After cooling to room temperature (25 ℃ ± 5 ℃), the mixture solution (light-yellow) was centrifuged and filtered sequentially to remove impurities. The supernatant was evaporated at 60 °C to get NCQD and its was stored away from light.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design and operation of experimental setup for photo-AD
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, the photo-AD experimental device was modified based on the Automatic Methane Potential Test System II (AMPTSII ®) (BPC Instruments, Sweden). The fermentation unit was a 100 mL serum bottle (Fig. 1 (a)), which was constructed by wrapping tin paper to isolate light (Fig. 1(b) and (d)). The photo-AD system was constructed by wrapping LED lamp (20 W) around the bottle (Fig. 1(c)). The total power provided by the simulated light source is 250 W/m2 (Fig. 1(e)). The experiment was implemented with R (dark-AD without NCQD), RNCQD (dark-AD with 0.5 g/L NCQD), RL (photo-AD without NCQD), and RL NCQD (photo-AD with 0.5 g/L NCQD). For each condition, three parallel groups were set up. Before the experiment, N2 was introduced into the gas path to create an anaerobic environment. During the experiment, 12 h of light were provided to the fermentation unit every day. The fermentation unit was maintained at 120 rpm and 37 ± 2 °C, and connected to an absorption bottle containing NaOH solution through a hose. Biogas was purified by passing through the absorption bottle to remove CO2 and H2S. After purification, the generated CH4 was recorded by a gas meter unit. The gas flow recording data was presented through an online analysis module (Bioprocess Control gas Endeavour).

Transmission electron microscope (TEM, FEI Tecnai G2 F20, US) and atomic force microscope (AFM, Bruker Dimension ICON, Germany) were used to analyze the morphology and size of materials. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurement was performed via Thermo ESCALA 250Xi spectrometer system using Al K-α radiation. The UV–visible diffuse reflectance spectrum of the sample was recorded on a PE Lambda (US) to measure the absorbance. The photoluminescence (PL) spectrum was detected using the lifetime and steady state spectrometer (FLS 980, UK).

The modified Gompertz mathematical equation (kinetics models) was used to evaluate AD system:
(1)
B =
B 0
× e x p
- e x p
e ×
μ m
×
( λ - t )
B 0
+ 1

Where, B is the cumulative CH4 yield for the digestion time t (mL/g VS), B0 is ultimate CH4 yield of (mL/g VS), μm is the maximum yield rate (mL/g VS/day), λ is the lag time (days), t is the digestion time (days), and e is a natural constant.

The analysis of microbial community structure follows our previous method [22]. adenosine triphosphate (ATP) test methods were based on the assay kit purchased from Wuhan Biowefind Biotechnology Co., Ltd and used according to manufacturer’s instructions. In brief, 0.1 mL reaction mixture (digestate liquid) was homogenized with 1.0 mL of extracting solution 1 (PBS). After centrifugation at 12,000 rpm for 10 min at 4 °C, the supernatant was homogenized with 1.0 mL of extracting solution 2 and then used for measuring ATP. After heating in a water bath at 37 °C for 20 min, the liquid was transferred to 96-well plates, and read using a microplate reader (MolecularDevices SpectraMax ABS plus, US) at OD700 nm.

Quantitative polymerase chain reaction (qPCR) was used to analyze the content of specific genes (EchA, VhtA, HdrD). The experimental process involves extracting DNA/RNA, designing, synthesizing, and debugging primers (Table S1), and conducting absolute qPCR analysis (SYBR Green method). The gene copynumber were calculated based on the standard curve (Table S2) and the obtained Ct (Cycle threshold) value (Eq. (2) and (3)):
(2) Ct = k·logCopynumber + b
(3) Copynumber = 10(Ct-b)/
k

Electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectroscopy was obtained using the A300 (Bruker, US) instrument to analyze •OH. The fermentation substance was stained by SYTO 9 (Ex = 488/Em = 498) / PI (580 ∼ 700 nm), and then the cell integrity was observed by confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM, Leica TCS SP8, Germany).

Before conducting electrochemical testing, the sample was coated to the surface of the conductive glass with indium tin oxide (ITO, 10 mm × 10 mm) side and dried at room temperature (25 ± 2 °C) for 30 ∼ 60 min. All the analyses were conducted via an electro-chemical workstation (CHI660E, Chenhua Inc., Shanghai China) with Ag/AgCl and Pt flat as the reference and counter electrode, respectively. The cyclic voltammetry (CV) test with a scanning rate of 10 mV/s was conducted in the potential area of −0.7 to 0.8 V. Conductive glass coated with samples was used as the working electrode for electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) testing at 0.01 V. I-t curve analysis was performed using an LED lights (50 W) as light sources with on–off 10 s/10 s.

The data on methane yield and gene content in this study were represented as mean and standard deviation (STDEV). Statistical analysis was conducted using one-way ANOVA and Student's T-test, and a p-value of < 0.05 was considered statistically significant. The data statistics and presentation software used in this study were Origin 2021 (Origin Lab, US) and Excel 2021 (Microsoft, US).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Physicochemical characteristics and photochemical properties of NCQD
ce:section.ce:para: The NCQD synthesized through the hydrothermal method exhibited uniformly distributed in the solution with an average particle size of 1.9 ± 0.5 nm (Fig. 2 (a) ∼ (c)). In addition, HRTEM images showed that NCQD possessed a 0.20 nm interplanar distance, which was a typical (100) plane of graphite carbon ring units (Fig. 2(d) ∼ (e)) [23]. The crystalline characteristics indicated that NCQD has been successfully prepared. Furthermore, AFM results showed that NCQD had a radial height (2.6 nm, 2.5 nm, 1.5 nm) similar to its diameter, indicating that NCQD exhibited a nearly spherical shape (Fig. S1). These morphological features were consistent with the characteristics of CQD [24]. Fig. S2 showed that the XPS spectrum of NCQD possessed three characteristic peaks, at 284 eV (C 1 s), 401 eV (N 1 s) and 531 eV (O 1 s), respectively [25]. After fitting the peaks of C 1 s, three high-resolution peaks of C-C, C-N, and C = O were obtained (Fig. 2(g)), which were located at 284.8 eV, 286.4 eV and 288.3 eV respectively [26,27]. Similarly, N 1 s fitting obtained the characteristic peaks of pyridine N (398.3 eV), pyrrole N (399.7 eV) and graphite N (401.3 eV) (Fig. 2(h)) [28,29]. The presence of N bonds in the XPS spectrum indicated that N elements have been doped into CQD.

NCQD is a material with visible photo-response [30]. Fig. 3 (a) and 3(b) showed that the maximum absorption wavelength of NCQD was 360 nm, and it had intense absorption under visible light (>420 nm). When a visible light source occurred, the electrode coated with NCQD raised a rapid current response (Fig. 3(b)). Thereby, although NCQD possessed fluorescence (Fig. S3), the separation efficiency of electron-hole pairs was still acceptable under visible light irradiation. Meanwhiles, the lower valence band energy indicated that the conduction band energy of NCQD was negative (Fig. S3(b)), which was sufficient for transferring the photo-generated electrons to the C-cytochrome (OmcA: −25 to − 325 mV vs SHE; MtrC: −0.5 to − 277.5 mV vs SHE) or other electron receiving units [31–33]. Additionally, the Mott-Schottky plot showed a positive slope, indicating that NCQD was an n-type semiconductor (Fig. 3(c)) [31]. Such semiconductors will generate more electrons when excited by light, rather than holes that have adverse effects on microorganisms. The low impedance was also conducive to the transfer of photo-generated electrons (Fig. 3(d)).

Both the cumulative and daily CH4 yield were recorded during the 12-day fermentation process. On day 1, dark-AD achieved higher CH4 yield than photo-AD, with 68.8 ± 18.0 mL/g VS (R) and 85.5 ± 34.5 mL/g VS (RNCQD) (Fig. 4 (a)), while that of photo-AD was 52.9 ± 21.6 mL/g VS (RL) and 28.8 ± 13.7 mL/g VS (RL NCQD), respectively. The maximum daily CH4 yield of all fermentation systems appeared in the first two days (Fig. 4(b)). The lower CH4 yield for photo-AD may be attributed to the adaptation period of microorganisms in the photo environment. After that, photo-AD system exhibited superior methanogenic performance. On day 2, the cumulative CH4 yield of the photo-AD rapidly increased. In RL NCQD, for instance, the cumulative CH4 yield reached 107.8 ± 30.4 mL/g VS, 17.7 % increments compared to RNCQD. In addition, the maximum daily CH4 yield of RL NCQD reached a maximum value of 78.9 ± 26.1 mL/g VS on day 2, which was higher than the dark-AD (75.3 ± 25.1 mL/g VS). The cumulative CH4 yield of photo-AD (RL) exceeded dark-AD on day 4, indicating that although light exposure significantly promoted the AD process even an adaption period (lag phase) was needed. This was further confirmed by the model simulation. As shown in Table S3, photo-AD had a larger λ (0.14 ∼ 0.21 days > 0.09 ∼ 0.12 days) and μm (24.26 ∼ 36.68 mL/g VS/day > 25.70 ∼ 29.71 mL/g VS/day). It was worth noting that in the photo-AD system, the daily CH4 yield peaked multi-times (Fig. 4(b)). The recurrence could be attributed to the different CH4 production rate between light/dark cycles, which further support the view that NCQD under a light environment have a promoting effect on methanogenesis [8]. As shown in Fig. 4(c), photo-AD obtained a significantly higher CH4 yield (89.8–96.5 %) compared to dark-AD. In dark-AD system, NCQD did not promote the cumulative CH4 yield significantly. In photo-AD, however, NCQD promoted CH4 yield to 258.17 ± 26.17 mL/g VS, which was 23.1 % higher than that from RL. The positive effect of NCQD under light condition indicated that it can promote methanogenesis through potential photo-response [18,34,35].

To reveal the effects of light and optoelectronic material on the microbial community structure, the 16 s rRNA of all groups was analyzed (Fig. 5 (a) ∼ (b) and Fig. S4). The Chao and Shannon index showed that the species richness significantly increased after giving light and NCQD (Table S4) [36]. Moreover, the decrease in Simpson index after providing light indicated that light enhanced species uniformity in the system [37]. These results indicated that illumination and optoelectronic materials contribute to improving abundance and uniformity.

Further analysis of the microbial abundance at different levels showed that the abundance of Firmicutes was at a higher level in all groups (Fig. S4(a)). At the class level of bacteria, Clostridia and Bacilli were the dominant strains (Fig. 5(a)). In the group RL, the total abundance of these two strains reached 87.9 %. In the group without light, the abundance of these two strains was 74.1 % (RNCQD) and 73.5 % (R), respectively. Interestingly, the total abundance of Clostridia and Bacilli did not increase (69.5 %) in RL NCQD, but 18.1 % of Thermoanaerobium was detected. The emergence of this strain may be due to NCQD absorbing light and improving the system temperature. Research has shown that Thermoanaerobacteria has glycolysis ability, which contributed to promoting AD [38]. The increased bacteria abundance related to hydrolysis provided more sufficient substrates (acetic acid etc.) for methanogenesis. Fig. 5(b) showed that light exposure significantly altered the community structure of archaea. The dominant strain in the dark-AD was Methanobacteriales, while in photo-AD, that was dominated by Methanobacteriales and Methanosarciniales. Moreover, the addition of NCQD further shifted the dominant strain towards Methanosarciniales (69.0 %). Compared with RL, the dominant strain increased by 11.3 %. At the order level, these strains were further identified as Methanosaeta (42.0 %) and Methanosarcina (27 %). These results indicated that NCQD increases the abundance of Methanosarciniales under light conditions. According to reports, Methanosarciniales (Methanosarcina, Methanosaeta, Methanobacterium, etc.) could receive electrons through DITE, while conductive materials could promote DIET to strengthen methanogenesis [39,40]. Therefore, NCQD probably further increased methane production through promoted DIET under light conditions. Furthermore, considering the photoelectrochemical properties of NCQD, it was highly likely that NCQD formed biotic–abiotic interfaces with microorganisms and increased methane production through supplied photoelectrons. At present, the biological–abiotic hybrid photocatalytic system has been confirmed, and methanogen–semiconductor hybrid system has been built for light-driven CO2-to-CH4 [9,41].

However, photocatalytic materials will generate reactive oxygen species (ROS), such as O2 •–, 1O2, and •OH, when stimulated by light. Excessive free radicals will lead to microbial apoptosis [42]. During the AD process, O2 •– and 1O2 was theoretically not generated due to the absence of oxygen, but •OH still be generated. As shown in Fig. 5(c), four characteristic peaks with a strong intensity of 1:2:2:1 (*) appeared, indicating the generation of •OH in the system [43]. The quantitative analysis found that the •OH was only 3.537 × 10-6 mol/L (0.06 mg/L), which was insufficient to bring a significant kill ratio [44]. The imaging results demonstrated the structural integrity of microorganisms in the light-AD (Fig. S5) and the number of live cells was almost consistent with that of dark-AD (Fig. 5(d) ∼ (e)). The findings revealed that the radicals did not induce microbial apoptosis in photo-AD within the scope of this study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The mechanism of NCQD on photo-AD promotion
ce:section.ce:para: To further explore the functional mechanism of NCQD in the photo-AD system, photoelectrochemical and molecular biological characteristics were analyzed. CV analysis showed that all curves had redox peaks (0.22 V and −0.40 V), even in the system (R) solely encompassed microorganisms (Fig. 6 (a)). It was evident that microorganisms within AD exhibited the capability to facilitate electron transfer not only via direct interspecies electron transfer (DIET) as indicated by the oxidation peak but also through electron transfer, as highlighted by the reduction peak [19,20]. The pathway of mediate electron transfer by electron shuttles (such as riboflavin) has also been confirmed [20]. Notably, the NCQD in dark-AD did not enhance the intensity of the redox peak. After upon light exposure, there was an increase in the intensity of the reduction peak, indicating that light promoted the mediate electron transfer pathway. Upon introduction of NCQD in the photo-AD system, the intensity of the redox peak further increased, accompanied by a notable expansion in the CV curve area. These results indicated that the NCQD-microorganism hybrid has more redox species, significantly improving the extracellular electron transfer (EET) [45,46]. The increase in EET level was further confirmed by impedance measurements. As shown in Fig. 6(b), the impedance was markedly diminished in the system containing NCQD. It should be pointed out that the hybrid also has lower impedance in RNCQD; however, the lack of light excitation prevented the generation of active species.

Apart from promoting EET, NCQD can also supply photoelectrons to microorganisms. The I-t curve showed (Fig. 6(c)) that the photocurrent intensity of RL did not change during the light/dark cycle, indicating that microorganisms did not have photo-response. Conversely, upon the addition of NCQD, the photocurrent generated instantaneous changes during the light and dark cycle, indicating that photoelectrons were quickly absorbed by microorganisms. The photocurrent intensity of RL NCQD was higher than NCQD, which may be attributed to the rapid absorption of photoelectrons by electron acceptors on the surface of microbial cell membranes (such as EPS) [47,48].

An increase in electron content and transfer level is typically accompanied by an raised ATP content[49]. Fig. 6(d) showed that the experimental group provided with light had higher ATP concentration compared to dark-AD. The ATP concentration was 0.22 ± 0.03 μmol/mL in RL, which was higher than dark-AD (R). This can be explained by the additional radiation heat generated by light which increased microbial activity, even light exposure alone cannot generate photoelectrons [10]. In the photo-AD system, NCQD further increased the ATP concentration by 18.2 % (0.26 ± 0.03 μmol/mL). However, the increased ATP concentration cannot be solely attributed to the conversion of photoelectrons. Another possible reason was the positive effect of microbial uptake NCQD. Previous studies have shown the increased ATP concentration levels of Shewanella oneidensis MR-1 with NCQD uptake [20].

Photoelectrons and interspecific electron transfer depend on the electron‐transfer chain (ETC) of microbial systems, which is composed of functional proteins [50,51]. Therefore, specific DNA was amplified by qPCR to determine the effects of light exposure and NCQD on electron transfer pathways. Fig. 6(e) showed that the total DNA concentration in photo-AD was higher. Addition of NCQD further upgraded DNA concentration to 10.9 ± 1.4 ng/μL, representing a higher level of microbial abundance. These results were consistent with the microbiological analysis (Table S4). In particular, utilization of photoelectrons depends on membrane-bound hydrogenase ([NiFe]‐hydrogenase, Ech; Methanophenazine-dependent hydrogenase, Vht, Heterodisulfide reductase, Hdr), which were key component of the ETC [8,52,53]. Research showed that Ech was an indispensable enzyme for methanogenesis of CO2, and its function was to receive electrons for the reduction of CO2 to formylmethanofuran [8]. In the absence of Ech, methanogen (Methanosarcina barkeri) could not grow on methanol-H2 [54]. The qPCR results (Fig. 6(f)) showed that the EchA concentration was high in the AD system. In RL NCQD, the gene copynumber of EchA exceeded 60,000 g−1 (61,652.8 g−1). NCQD significantly increased the copynumber of EchA genes in photo-AD, while NCQD did not increase in dark-AD. For this reason, it can be seen that NCQD improved electron supply levels by providing photoelectrons, leading to an increase in EchA concentration, rather than the effect of microbial uptake NCQD. Another electronic transfer method was to use cytochrome. Research has shown that methanogens without cytochromes did not encode Vht and Hdr. Fig. S6 showed that light and the NCQD did not significantly improve their expression. This result indicated that NCQD in photo-AD has no significant effect on the electron conduction via cytochrome. Research has shown that pili composed of cytochrome were used to complete DIET. Therefore, although NCQD has two potential ways to promote electron transfer in photo-AD, direct absorption of photoelectrons was the main path. This conclusion was sufficient to support previous experimental observations, including the increase in the abundance of dominant strains, ATP content, and the intensity of oxidation peaks in the CV curve.

The mechanism of NCQD in photo-AD was showed in Fig. 6(g). After being exposed to light, the electrons within NCQD transitioned from the valence band to the conduction band, rending NCQD a conductive. Meanwhiles, NCQD can promote methanogenesis by transferring electrons between species (such as bacteria and archaea). Additionally, the NCQD attached to the membrane of methanogen (Methanosarciniales) constructed a biotic-abiotic hybrid interface with the cell. Photoelectrons were transferred to the e- acceptor strain through Ech. These electrons were used for ATP and CH4 synthesis reactions. This visible light-driven methanogenesis was the dominant avenue for NCQD to improve methane yield in the photo-AD system.

In this study, NCQD was synthesized by hydrothermal method and utilized to enhance AD under light conditions. NCQD promoted methanogenesis in the photo-AD system, with an increase of 23.1 %. The improved CH4 yield was due to the higher abundance of Metanosarciniales (69.0 %) in the presence of NCQD. Moreover, the utilization of NCQD in photo-AD did not lead to an excessive generation of free radicals from NCQD, which poses no potential harm to microorganisms. The photoelectrochemical analysis results have shown that there were two potential roles of NCQD in photo-AD: (1) Promoting EET; (2) Generating photoelectrons for methanogenesis. Notably, the latter mechanism emerged as the primary contributor for increasing methane production. This work heralds a groundbreaking strategy poised to revolutionize AD enhancement, while its revelatory insights cast a promising light on the future of sustainable waste management.

Qiang Liu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation, Investigation, Visualization, Writing – original draft. Shuo Chen: Investigation, Writing – review & editing. Haoyu Zhang: Investigation, Writing – review & editing. Lu Feng: Visualization, Writing – review & editing. Hongjun Zhou: Visualization, Validation, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing. Junting Pan: Validation, Writing – review & editing. Yeqing Li: Conceptualization, Supervision, Visualization, Validation, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing. Chunming Xu: Visualization, Validation, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22278435), the Strategic Cooperation Technology Projects of CNPC and CUPB (ZLZX2020-04), the Project of the Joint Research Institute of Carbon Neutrality, State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil Processing (CUPB-SH-2-02).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2023.146359.

ce:section.ce:para:

Malvankar, Microbial biofilms as living photoconductors due to ultrafast electron transfer in cytochrome OmcS nanowires, Nat. Commun. 13(1) (2022) 5150. https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-022-32659-5.
